title-group.article-title: A novel Notch and WNT signaling mechanism contribute to pediatric DCM: a pathway to new therapeutics

article-meta.aff: 1 Integrative Physiology Graduate Program, University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus, Aurora, CO 80045, USA.
2 Department of Medicine, Division of Cardiology, University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus, Aurora, CO 80045, USA.
3 University of Utah Department of Internal Medicine.
4 Department of Medicine, Division of General Internal Medicine, University of Colorado School of Medicine, Aurora, CO, 80045.
5 Bioengineering Department, University of Colorado Denver Anschutz Medical Campus, Aurora, Colorado, 80045.
6 Department of Pediatrics, Division of Cardiology, University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus, Aurora, CO 80045, USA.

fn.p: OON performed experiments, data analysis and interpretation, manuscript writing and editing. ER performed experiments, data analysis and manuscript writing. JG performed experiments and data analysis. AKF performed statistical and bioinformatics analyses. CC performed some experiments. LHL performed experiments, MPAB performed data analysis. BP performed experiments and guided AFM analysis. SDM was involved in experimental design, project supervision, clinical data interpretation, and manuscript writing and editing. BLS was involved in experimental design, project supervision, data interpretation, and manuscript writing and editing. CCS conceptualized the study, was involved in experimental design, data analysis and interpretation, manuscript writing and editing. All authors reviewed and approved the manuscript.

author-notes.corresp: # Corresponding authors: Carmen C. Sucharov. 12700 E. 19th Ave., B-139 Aurora, Aurora, CO 80045, USA. kika.sucharov@cuanschutz.edu, Brian Stauffer. 12700 E. 19th Ave., B-139 Aurora, Aurora, CO 80045, USA. brian.stauffer@cuanschutz.edu, Shelley Miyamoto. 12700 E. 19th Ave., B-139 Aurora, Aurora, CO 80045, USA. Shelley.miyamoto@childrenscolorado.org

license.license-p: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which allows reusers to copy and distribute the material in any medium or format in unadapted form only, for noncommercial purposes only, and only so long as attribution is given to the creator.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy (iDCM) in children is a life-threatening disease. Little is known about its cellular and transcriptional landscape, and the lack of disease-specific animal models limits our understanding of its mechanisms. We previously demonstrated that pediatric iDCM serum-circulating proteins promote pathologic remodeling in vitro, and that secreted frizzled related protein 1 (sFRP1) increases stiffness in cardiomyocytes. Here we investigated the mechanisms by which sFRP1 contributes to cardiac dysfunction.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The effect of sFRP1 in combination with isoproterenol (ISO) (to recapitulate the increase in circulating catecholamine observed in pediatric DCM patients) was evaluated in neonatal rat ventricular myocytes (in vitro), and in neonatal rats through intraperitoneal injections (in vivo). Function and molecular mechanisms were investigated through echocardiography and next-generation-sequencing respectively. Protein levels and localization were determined by Western blot. Tissue stiffness was measured by Atomic Force Microscopy. In vitro and in vivo data were compared to explanted human heart tissue (ex vivo).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: We show that ISO+sFRP1 reactivates the fetal gene program in vitro, and promotes cardiac dysfunction, dilation and stiffness in vivo. Importantly, we show stiffness is also increased in pediatric iDCM hearts. We identified co-activation of Notch and WNT signaling in both ISO+sFRP1-treated rats and pediatric iDCM hearts. Mechanistically, in vitro inhibition of Notch or β-catenin prevented pathological remodeling, and Notch inhibition improved cardiac function and reduced ventricular dilation in ISO+sFRP1-treated rats and NRVMs.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: We identified concordant alterations in Notch and WNT signaling in pediatric iDCM hearts and in our ISO+sFRP1-treated rats. Notch inhibition abrogated pathologic changes in vitro and in vivo. These findings provide novel mechanistic insights and a potential therapeutic target for pediatric iDCM.

award-group.funding-source: NIH, Colorado CTSA Grant, Jack Cooper Millisor Chair in Pediatric Heart Disease, American Heart Association (AHA) supplement to promote diversity in science, AHA predoctoral fellowship, Ludeman Family Center for Women’s Health Research, American Heart Association, John Patrick Albright

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy (iDCM) is one of the main causes of cardiac-related mortality in children. Pediatric iDCM is a rare disease, making it very difficult to perform large, randomized placebo-controlled clinical trials (RCTs) in this population. The first RCT performed in children with heart failure (HF) studied the non-selective β-adrenergic receptor (β–AR) blocker, carvedilol. Importantly, although carvedilol has been shown to decrease morbidity and mortality in adults with DCM, the results of the trial were neutral in children1, even though there are similarities in the pathophysiology of HF in children and adults, including increased circulating catecholamines2,3. Unfortunately, to date, there is limited evidence to support the treatment of HF in children, and current therapies are largely based on extrapolation of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for adult patients without proven efficacy in children.

Existing animal models of HF in children tend to focus on specific genetic mutations. However, only about one-third of children with iDCM have any identified pathogenic genetic variant4, making use of any single genetic model difficult to generalize to the pediatric iDCM population. The lack of a generalizable animal model has negatively impacted the ability to investigate mechanisms of disease and identify targeted therapeutic options, which likely contributes to the fact that pediatric HF outcomes lag behind those of adults5. There is, however, a growing appreciation that molecular and cellular pathologic remodeling in pediatric HF is unique and lacks the well-known phenotypic changes observed in the adult heart6. We and others have shown that pediatric iDCM hearts have minimal fibrosis5,7, lack cardiomyocyte hypertrophy5,8, display an age-specific transcriptome profile5,8, unique changes to the β–AR system9, and diminished immune cell response5.

In the pathological state, the heart responds by reverting to the fetal gene program (FGP)10, which includes re-expression of natriuretic peptides (NPPA/NPPB) and reduced expression of the ratio α/β-Myosin Heavy Chain (MHC–Myh6/Myh7)10,11. We tested the effect of the pediatric iDCM secretome on gene expression and cellular stiffness in primary cardiomyocytes and showed that circulating proteins contribute to pathologic changes in cells12. We further showed that the protein secreted frizzled protein 1 (sFRP1) is increased in the serum and hearts of patients with iDCM and promoted cellular stiffness in primary cardiomyocytes12. sFRP1 is known to modulate the WNT signaling pathway13. WNT ligands regulate cellular activities through the activation of β-catenin-dependent (canonical) and independent (non-canonical) pathways13. The WNT signaling pathway influences Notch signaling activity in various cellular and developmental contexts. In cardiogenesis, both WNT and Notch signaling pathways are crucial in determining cell fate and survival14,15. The activity of Notch signaling is usually reduced in the postnatal heart but increased in cases of damage or injury16. Activation of the Notch receptors and subsequent cleavage by ADAM metalloproteinases and γ-secretase leads to the release of the Notch intracellular domain (NICD) in the cytoplasm, which translocates to the nucleus and activates transcription of Notch target genes resulting in cell specific effects14.

In this study we investigated the effect of sFRP1 in vitro and in vivo through the development of a novel animal model that consists of intraperitoneal injections of sFRP1 and isoproterenol (to recapitulate the increase in circulating catecholamine observed in pediatric DCM patients)2,3. We show that this model reproduces aspects of pediatric iDCM, including decreased cardiac function associated with dilation, and activation of Notch and WNT signaling. We further show that ISO+sFRP1 promotes cardiac stiffness, and importantly, inhibiting Notch signaling prevents pathological remodeling and cardiac dysfunction.

sec.title: Human Samples
sec.p: Detailed demographics from all patients included in the study are depicted in Supplemental Table 1. Median age for DCM patients was 3.9 years (interquartile range-IQR=6.2), with an average EF of 17.33%, 58.3% were female, 8.3% were on beta-blockers, all were on angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEi) and 75% were on a phosphodiesterase 3-inhibitor (PDE3i). Non-failing control (NF) subject had a median age of 6.9 years (IQR=9.5), 28.6% were female and 14.3% were on beta-blockers.

sec.title: sFRP1 and ISO treatment promotes pathological remodeling in vitro
sec.p: We first evaluated if treatment with ISO and/or sFRP1 promoted pathological remodeling in vitro. NRVMs were treated with ISO, sFRP1 or a combination of both factors for 72 hours. Fetal gene program (FGP), as measured by upregulation of NPPA, NPPB, and a decrease in the ratio of α-myosin heavy chain (MYH6) to β-myosin heavy chain (MYH7), was observed only when cells were treated with ISO+sFRP1. NPPA increased in both sFRP1 (p=0.0379) and ISO+sFRP1-treated cells (p<0.0001), NPPB increased in ISO (p<0.0001) and ISO+sFRP1-treated cells (p<0.0001), while MYH6/MYH7 decreased in response to sFRP1 (p<0.0001) and ISO+sFRP1 treatments (p=0.0142) (Fig. 1a). sFRP1 expression increased in cells treated with ISO+sFRP1 (p=0.0111) (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, the increase in sFRP1 gene expression in ISO+sFRP1-treated cells positively correlated with NPPA (p=0.0004) and NPPB expression (p<0.0001) and negatively correlated with MYH6/MYH7 (p<0.0001) (Supp. Fig. 1). These results suggest that a combination of ISO and sFRP1 promote pathological remodeling in vitro.

sec.title: sFRP1 and ISO treatment promotes cardiac dysfunction and dilation in a young rodent model
sec.p: To further validate the translational relevance of the in vitro model, we tested the effect of ISO+sFRP1 in vivo. Rats were injected with ISO and/or sFRP1 intraperitoneally for 2 weeks (Fig. 1c). Rats injected with ISO+sFRP1 had significantly reduced ejection fraction (EF) (p<0.0001) when compared to vehicle-treated control rats, as well as when compared to rats injected solely with ISO (p=0.0316) or sFRP1 (p<0.0001) (Fig. 1d). Echocardiogram findings also showed that left ventricular volume in systole (LV vol(s)) increased in ISO+sFRP1-treated rats compared to vehicle-treated controls (p=0.0002), ISO-treated rats (p=0.0306) or sFRP1-treated rats (p=0.0006) (Fig. 1e). Similarly, left ventricular internal diameter in systole (LVID(s)) increased in ISO+sFRP1 rats compared to vehicle-treated controls (p<0.0001), ISO-treated rats (p=0.0334) or sFRP1-treated rats (p<0.0001) (Fig. 1f). There were no significant differences in left ventricular anterior and posterior wall thickness for any of the treated groups (Fig. 1g, h). Diastolic parameters, LV vol(d) and LVID(d), followed the same trend as systolic parameters (Supp Fig. 2a, b). Morphometric data showed a significant increase in whole heart to body weight and LV weight to body weight ratios in ISO+sFRP1 rats compared to vehicle-treated controls (Supp. Fig. 2c, d). Functional alterations were not restricted to male animals; changes were also observed in females (Supp Fig. 2e).

As in the in vitro expression studies, NPPA expression increased in sFRP1− (p=004) and ISO+sFRP1-treated rats (p=0.0035) compared to vehicle-treated controls (Supp. Fig 3a). However, NPPB expression only increased in sFRP1-treated rats (p=0.0011) and not in response to ISO+sFRP1 treatment (Supp. Fig 3b). MYH6/MYH7 ratio decreased in both sFRP1 (p=0.0272) and ISO+sFRP1 rats (p=0.049) (Supp. Fig 3c). A simple linear regression was performed to evaluate the relationship between pathological cardiac remodeling, evidenced by reactivation of the FGP, and ejection fraction. We found that the relative expression of NPPA, but not NPPB, was significantly correlated with a decrease in EF (p=0.0039) (Supp. Fig 4a), whereas the ratio of MYH6/MYH7 trended towards a positive correlation (higher MYH6/MYH7 correlated with higher EF) (p=0.0825) (Supp. Fig 4b). These results, together with the in vitro data, provide evidence that ISO+sFRP1 promotes pathological remodeling in vitro and cardiac dysfunction associated with left ventricular dilation in vivo.

To investigate the translational significance of the rat ISO+sFRP1 DCM model, we investigated cardiac mRNA and serum sFRP1 levels in rats relative to our published data in human pediatric DCM patients8,12. We observed a significant increase in cardiac sFRP1 mRNA levels in ISO+sFRP1 rats (p=0.0003) (Fig. 2a) that was negatively correlated with EF (p=0.0121) (Supp. Fig 5a). Moreover, levels of sFRP1 in the serum of ISO+sFRP1 rats were significantly higher than vehicle-treated controls (Fig. 2b) (p=0.0146). There was a trend to negative correlation when analyzing levels of serum circulating sFRP1 and EF (p=0.0888) in rats (Supp. Fig 5b). The observed increase in rat cardiac and serum sFRP1 levels was comparable to our previously published human data12.

sec.title: ISO+sFRP1 promotes increased stiffness in the absence of myocyte hypertrophy or fibrosis.
sec.p: We previously showed that DCM serum or sFRP1 treatment promotes stiffness in NRVMs assessed by atomic force microscopy (AFM)12. To further explore if sFRP1 promotes stiffness in vivo, we investigated myocardial stiffness in rats. As shown in Fig. 2c–e, ISO+sFRP1 promoted a significant increase in LV stiffness when compared to vehicle-treated control rats (p=0.02). Stiffness is specific to cardiac dysfunction as changes in myocardial stiffness were not observed in rats treated with ISO only or sFRP1 only (Supp. Fig. 6a–d). Importantly, we tested stiffness in the LV of children with DCM and show that stiffness is increased when compared to NF healthy controls (p=0.0176) (Fig. 2f–h).

We and others previously showed that pediatric DCM patients have minimal fibrosis and lack myocyte hypertrophy5,17. Compared to vehicle-treated controls, ISO+sFRP1 rats did not exhibit increased fibrosis, as determined by Masson’s trichrome staining (Supp. Fig. 7a–c). Additionally, RT-qPCR analysis of the rat LV tissue demonstrated no significant upregulation of profibrotic genes, including COL1A1, COL3A1, Galectin-3, TGFB1, TGFB2 and TIMP1–4 (Supp. Fig 7d–l). Furthermore, myocyte hypertrophy was not observed in ISO+sFRP1 rats compared to controls (Supp. Fig.7m–o).

sec.title: Notch signaling is altered in ISO+sFRP1 rats and in pediatric DCM hearts.
sec.p: To better understand the pathophysiology of ISO+sFRP1-mediated DCM and identify the mechanisms underlying the disease, we performed bulk RNA sequencing analysis of the LV of rats treated with ISO and/or sFRP1 compared to vehicle-treated controls (n=6/group) (Fig. 3a). Of the 14,350 genes identified, there were 286 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) (p<0.05) in ISO+sFRP1 rats compared to vehicle-treated controls (113 up-regulated and 173 down-regulated) (Supp. Table 2). Unsupervised hierarchical clustering separated control and ISO+sFRP1 treatment groups based on the gene expression profile. Unsupervised hierarchical cluster heatmaps of the top 50 DEGs in ISO+sFRP1− (Fig. 3b), sFRP1− or ISO-treated rats (supp. Fig. 8a, b) compared to vehicle-treated controls were generated and showed a clear separation between individual groups and control. Pathways were analyzed using the identified significantly upregulated or downregulated genes. Pathway analysis of upregulated genes highlighted Notch signaling pathway as one of the main dysregulated pathways associated with upregulated genes uniquely enriched in response to ISO+sFRP1 compared to controls (Fig. 3c). WNT/β-catenin signaling was the 2nd top pathway for all dysregulated DEGs in ISO+sFRP1 rats (Fig. 3d). Pathway analysis was also done for downregulated genes (Supp. Fig. 8c), and for ISO− or sFRP1-treated rats. The WNT/β-catenin signaling pathway was significantly dysregulated in sFRP1-treated rats while mitochondrial organization pathways were dysregulated in ISO-treated rats (Supp. Fig. 8d–g)

Importantly, in our prior investigations of explanted pediatric DCM hearts, we identified changes in gene expression that suggested alterations in the Notch and WNT pathways8. To investigate commonalities in hearts from children with DCM and our rat model, we assessed similarities between DEGs from ISO+sFRP1 rats, pediatric DCM hearts, and from our previously published data on pediatric DCM serum-treated NRVMs12. Transcriptomic analysis revealed a substantial overlap in DEGs in the LV tissue of ISO+sFRP1-treated rats and human DCM hearts or serum-treated cells. Notably, 96% of DEGs in the LV tissue of ISO+sFRP1-treated rats were also observed in the human DCM heart (65% unique only to human LV DEGs, 31% shared between human LV and rat LV DEGs with only 4% DEGs unique to rats) (Fig. 3e). Additionally, 57% of DEGs were common between the LV tissue of ISO+sFRP1-treated rats and NRVMs treated with human DCM serum (57% unique only to NRVMs treated with human DCM serum DEGs, 1% shared between NRVMs treated with human DCM serum DEGs and rat LV DEGs with 42% DEGs unique to rats) (Fig. 3f). Again, Notch signaling was one of the top dysregulated pathways associated with commonly upregulated genes in DCM serum-treated NRVMs and LV tissue of ISO+sFRP1-treated rats (Fig. 3g).

sec.title: Single-nuclei RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) identifies activation of Notch and β-catenin signaling in response to ISO+sFPR1
sec.p: To further characterize the cell types and biological mechanisms altered at the individual cellular level, we performed single-nuclei RNA sequencing of LV tissue from ISO and/or sFRP1, and vehicle-treated control rats (n=3/group). A total of 9,214 nuclei in control, 11,588 nuclei in ISO−, 13,758 nuclei in sFRP1−, and 11,127 in ISO+sFRP1-treated rats were identified after cells were pooled. Clusters were annotated based on canonical cell marker expression (supp. Table 3). We assessed the distribution of cell-type annotated nuclei across all samples and identified 26 transcriptionally distinct cell-type clusters marked by canonical gene markers (Fig. 4a)18,19. The proportions of the different identified cell types varied across treatment groups. Representation of the various cell type distribution showed an increase in the relative ratio of ventricular cardiomyocytes in the ISO+sFRP1-treated rats (Fig. 4b, c, supp. Table 3). A sub-analysis of ventricular cardiomyocytes identified eight different sub-clusters (Fig. 4d). Pathway analysis of dysregulated DEGs in the ISO+sFRP1 ventricular cardiomyocyte subcluster 2 (the cluster with the most relative proportion of ventricular cardiomyocytes) suggests activation of Notch signaling as one of the top dysregulated pathways (Fig. 4e), and CTNNB1 (β-catenin) as one of the top transcription factors predicted to control expression of upregulated genes (Fig. 4f). Additionally, pathway analysis suggests extracellular matrix (ECM) proteoglycan enrichment as the top dysregulated pathway in subcluster 6 (cluster with the 2nd most relative proportion of ventricular cardiomyocytes), and Notch1 as one of the top transcription factors predicted to control expression of dysregulated genes (Fig. 4g, h). We then used ClinVar to evaluate how DEGs in the ventricular cardiomyocyte cluster relate to human health, and identified DCM as the top phenotype, which further validates our model (Fig. 4i).

sec.title: Levels of Notch change in vivo, in vitro and in human DCM hearts.
sec.p: To further explore Notch signaling dysregulation in DCM, we investigated levels of Notch-target genes and Notch intracellular domain (NICD), the constitutively active form of Notch1, in the different models. We found increased expression of Notch target genes, which was associated with increased nuclear and cytoplasmic NICD. NICD protein was increased in cytoplasmic (p=0.0013) and nuclear (p=0.0143) fractions of pediatric DCM hearts (Fig. 5a–d), in cytoplasmic (p=0.0053) and nuclear fractions (p=0.0086) of ISO+sFRP1 rat hearts (Fig. 5e–h), and in cytoplasmic (p=0.0371) and nuclear (p =0.0119) fractions of ISO+sFRP1-treated NRVMs (Fig. 5i–l). In parallel, we also observed increased expression of Notch-target genes; Hey1, was upregulated in pediatric DCM hearts (1.55x, q=0.02 from RNA-seq data)8. Notch1 (p=0.0398) and HEY1 (p=0.0467) were up-regulated in ISO+sFRP1 rat hearts (Fig. 5m). Notch1 (p=0.0276) together with the expression of Notch target genes HEY1 (p=0.0006), HEY2 (p=0.0124), HES1 (p=0.0361) and HEYL (p=0.0436) were increased in NRVMs treated with ISO+sFRP1 compared to vehicle-treated controls (Fig. 5n). There was no increase in expression of other Notch receptor genes such as Notch2–4 (Supp. Fig. 9a–c). Expression of Notch 1 and Notch-target genes in ISO or sFRP1-treated NRVMs did not change (Supp. 10a–e).

sec.title: Levels of β-catenin change in vivo, in vitro and in human DCM hearts
sec.p: sFRP1 is known to modulate the β-catenin pathway in a context-dependent manner20. As shown in Fig. 3d our transcriptomic data strongly suggested the activation of the β-catenin pathway in ISO+sFRP1-treated rats, which is in agreement with our published explanted pediatric DCM heart data8. We tested expression of β-catenin gene (CTNNB1) and β-catenin protein levels in the cytoplasmic and nuclear fractions of explanted pediatric DCM hearts, ISO+sFRP1 rat hearts and ISO+sFRP1-treated NRVMS (Fig. 6). We found higher levels of β-catenin in the cytoplasmic (p=0.0013) and nuclear (p=0.0433) fractions in DCM hearts (Fig. 6a–d), in the cytoplasmic (p=0.0099) and nuclear fractions (p-value=0.0191) of ISO+sFRP1 rat hearts (Fig 6. e–h), and in the cytoplasmic (p=0.0101) and nuclear fractions (p=0.0039) of ISO+sFRP1-treated NRVMs (Fig 6. i–l). Additionally, we also observed an increase in expression of CTNNB1 (p=0.0263) in ISO+sFRP1 rats (Fig. 6m) and ISO+sFRP1-treated NRVMs (p=0.0460) (Fig. 6n). Similar to what we observed when evaluating Notch signaling, the increase in CTNNB1 levels was unique to ISO+sFRP1-treated NRVMs and was not observed in response to ISO or sFRP1. (Supp. Fig. 10f)

sec.title: β-catenin contributes to Notch activation in ISO+sFRP1-treated cells
sec.p: Others have reported an interplay between Notch and β-catenin signaling21,22. We next evaluated the contribution of β-catenin to Notch activation. siRNA-mediated down-regulation of CTNNB1 in ISO+sFRP1-treated cells prevented ISO+sFRP1-mediated activation of Notch target gene expression by blunting expression of HEY1 (p<0.0001), HEY2 (p=0.0026), HES1 (p=0.0167) and HEYL (p=0.0401) (Fig. 7a). Additionally, siRNA for CTNNB1 also prevented activation of the FGP by ISO+sFRP1 by blunting the increase in expression of NPPA (p=0.0063) and NPPB (p=0.0003), and by increasing the ratio of MYH6/MYH7 (p=0.0446) (Fig. 7b). siRNA for CTNNB1 also reduced the cytoplasmic (p=0.0041) (Fig. 7c, d) and nuclear (p=0.0063) (Fig. 7e, f) increase of NICD by ISO+sFRP1. The effect of si-CTNNB1 on β-catenin levels is shown in Fig. 8 c, e.

sec.title: Notch antagonism prevented pathological remodeling in vitro and cardiac dysfunction associated with dilation in vivo
sec.p: We also tested the effect of DAPT, a γ-secretase inhibitor known for reducing the levels of NICD, on β-catenin levels and activation of the FGP. DAPT, as expected, prevented or decreased the activation of Notch target genes HEY1 (p<0.0001), HEY2 (p=0.0004), HES1 (p<0.0001) and HEYL (p<0.0001) by ISO+sFRP1 (Fig. 8a). We next tested whether Notch inhibition affected activation of the FGP. As shown in Fig. 8b. DAPT prevented the reactivation of FGP by ISO+sFRP1 by blunting the increased expression of NPPA (P=0.025), and NPPB (P=0.0014), and by increasing the ratio of MYH6/MYH7 (p=0.007). Additionally, DAPT prevented ISO+sFRP1-mediated increase in cytoplasmic (p=0.0487) (Fig. 8c, d) and nuclear (p=0.0385) (Fig. 8e, f) β-catenin. DAPT also prevented the increase in CTNNB1 gene expression by ISO+sFRP1 (p=0.008) (Fig. 8g). These data support the premise that the co-activation of Notch and β-catenin promotes pathological remodeling.

Lastly, we investigated the role of DAPT in preventing cardiac dysfunction associated with ventricular dilation in our ISO+sFRP1 in vivo model. DAPT prevented the reduction of EF by ISO+sFRP1 (p=0.0048) and trended towards inhibiting the increase in LV vol(s) (p=0.088) and LVID(s) (p=0.0935) (Fig. 8h). These results suggest that Notch inhibition improves cardiac function in our rat model.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Studies focused on the mechanisms of HF in children are lacking. The rarity of the disease, associated with the difficulty in performing studies in a vulnerable population, has limited the development of age-specific therapies for pediatric iDCM, which contributes to a relatively high rate of mortality in this population23. Currently, there is no well-established disease model that accurately recapitulates the pathophysiology of pediatric iDCM. Importantly, pediatric and adult iDCM differ in several key aspects, including minimal myocardial fibrosis and no cardiomyocyte hypertrophy. These differences could be due to age-dependent mechanisms or to intrinsic differences in the disease process and may explain the differential response to medical therapies24. An optimal in vivo model should, therefore, mirror not only the echocardiographic and histological features but also the mechanistic characteristics of pediatric DCM. Our ISO+sFRP1 rat model fulfills these criteria, by exhibiting ventricular dilation, cardiac dysfunction, myocardial stiffness with minimal fibrosis, and activation of Notch-WNT signaling, closely paralleling the pediatric iDCM phenotype.

sec.title: sFRP1-mediated pathologic remodeling and cardiac dysfunction
sec.p: We have previously shown that circulating sFRP1 levels are elevated in pediatric DCM sera12. Likewise, catecholamines are increased in the serum of pediatric HF patients3. In this study we found that in vivo co-administration of ISO+sFRP1 in neonatal rats results in lower EF and LV dilation, and reactivation of the FGP. These findings suggest that β-adrenergic stimulation primes cardiomyocytes to revert to a fetal-like transcriptional state, which is a hallmark of maladaptive cardiac remodeling and HF and drives structural and functional cardiac dysfunction in vivo.

sec.title: sFRP1 regulation of WNT signaling
sec.p: sFRP1 is traditionally viewed as a WNT signaling antagonist, which reduces β-catenin levels. Our results suggest that sFRP1, in the context of β-adrenergic stimulation, acts as a WNT agonist rather than an antagonist as evidenced by the increased levels of β-catenin in ISO+sFRP1-treated cells and rats. Importantly, the increase in β-catenin levels was also observed in explanted pediatric DCM hearts. In support of our findings, other models of cardiac disease (such as myocardial infarction, cardiac fibrosis and HF) have highlighted the role of sFRP1 in activating WNT signaling in response to cardiac injury25.

Classically, sFRP1 inhibition of WNT signaling occurs relatively rapidly. Within the first 1–6 hours of treatment, sFRP1 inhibits WNT signaling by directly binding to WNT ligands or to frizzled receptors26. However, prolonged exposure and/or increased concentration of sFRP1 can promote β-catenin stabilization and WNT signaling activation27. Our results agree with these findings, as initial exposure to ISO+sFRP1 did not increase β-catenin levels (not shown), but persistent (72 hours) exposure increased levels of β-catenin in cardiomyocytes. The mechanisms associated with sFRP1-mediated stabilization of β-catenin are unclear; sFRP1 may directly form complexes with frizzled receptors, possibly mimicking the effects of WNT ligands or displacing inhibitory interactions, thus activating downstream signaling28. Furthermore, it has been proposed that the duration or levels of exposure to sFRP1 may stabilize certain WNT ligands or alter receptor dynamics to enhance β-catenin accumulation27.

Our results also suggest a context-dependent function of sFRP1, in which sFRP1 may differentially modulate WNT signaling under adrenergic stress. In cancer, chronic adrenergic stress has been shown to promote sFRP1 secretion, enhancing tumor growth through β-catenin activation27. Hence targeting sFRP1 has been explored as a potential therapeutic target for patients with adenocarcinoma29. Our findings extend these observations by demonstrating that simultaneous β-adrenergic stimulation and WNT pathway modulation can synergistically trigger maladaptive gene expression patterns and cardiac dysfunction, which may be unique to the pediatric population. In this context, ISO appears to act as a modulator of the response to sFRP1 and may modify the cellular context, allowing WNT signaling components to become activated despite the presence of a known antagonist, perhaps through non-canonical or compensatory mechanisms.

sec.title: WNT signaling in cardiac pathological remodeling
sec.p: WNT signaling is an essential pathway implicated in cell-cell communication and plays a role in cardiac development, function and disease from embryonic stages to adult life30. The WNT/β-catenin pathway has a biphasic role in cardiac development and differentiation, promoting cardiac differentiation before gastrulation and inhibiting cardiac development after gastrulation20. Additionally, although stabilizing β-catenin promotes proliferation of mammalian cardiomyocytes31, it is also required for stress-induced cardiac hypertrophy32. Furthermore, it has been suggested that active WNT/β-catenin signaling a week after cardiac injury is involved in cardiac healing33. In contrast, depletion of β-catenin attenuates ischemic cardiac remodeling through increased differentiation of resident cardiac progenitor cells34. These studies highlight the complexity of WNT/β-catenin signaling in the heart. In our study, we show that WNT/β-catenin contributes to a pathological phenotype, suggesting the activation of this pathway in post-natal hearts is associated with maladaptive remodeling and cardiac dysfunction.

sec.title: Notch and β-catenin axis promote DCM phenotype in a young rodent model
sec.p: Using transcriptomic data from our animal model, our data on molecular alterations in pediatric iDCM hearts and serum-treated NRVMs, we explored molecular pathways commonly altered in all models8,12.. Importantly, of the genes altered in ISO+sFRP1 rat hearts, the vast majority also changed in pediatric iDCM hearts (Fig. 3e). Additionally, pathway analysis of DEGs in ISO+sFRP1 rat hearts and in pediatric iDCM hearts8 highlight activation of Notch and WNT signaling. Recent evidence has suggested a crosstalk between the WNT and Notch signaling pathways in cardiac development and disease35. Notch signaling is fundamentally involved in cell-cell communication and has been implicated in cardiac development, function and cardiac birth defects36. Notch target genes contribute to cardiac development and disease37, and aberrations in the Notch signaling pathway are associated with congenital heart defects38. In addition, Notch signaling is activated after cardiac injury39, and promotes cardiac regeneration40, and cellular stiffness41. These suggest that Notch might be beneficial in regenerative, compliant environments such as the developing heart, but potentially detrimental in stiff, non-regenerative conditions such as the postnatal heart.

Prior studies have shown that both Notch and WNT pathways can stimulate proliferation of immature cardiomyocytes42 and promote cardiomyocyte survival43. Notch signaling has been shown to maintain the cardiac gene network needed for cardiac chamber formation through WNT signaling44. In the heart, both pathways are tightly regulated during embryogenesis, where they coordinate cardiac progenitor specification and morphogenesis40. However, in disease states, their reactivation often contributes to maladaptive remodeling45. Previous studies have shown that WNT/β-catenin signaling can enhance Notch pathway activation through direct transcriptional regulation of Notch ligands21,22 while Notch signaling has been reported to modulate β-catenin stability and nuclear translocation46. This bidirectional crosstalk may underlie the robust transcriptional and protein response we observed in our data. In summary, the relationship between Notch and WNT signaling is complex, dynamic, context dependent and is still being explored, but both pathways are known to play critical roles in cardiac development, homeostasis, and disease progression.

sec.title: The interplay between catecholamine and Notch and WNT signaling
sec.p: Interestingly, in our model, we observed an expansion of cardiomyocyte cluster 2 in ISO+sFRP1 hearts. In this cluster, there is an upregulation of Notch signaling, and CTNNB1 is predicted to be one of the top transcriptional regulators, supporting our findings that ISO+sFRP1 treatment results in WNT/Notch activation. Thus, the expansion of ventricular cardiomyocyte nuclei in cluster 2 may reflect both a compensatory survival mechanism and a shift toward a transcriptionally dysregulated state driven by concordant Notch-WNT pathway synergy. In congruence, ventricular cardiomyocyte cluster 6 predicts Notch1 as one of the top transcription factors predicted to control expression of upregulated genes. This suggests that one cluster may be more β-catenin-regulated and the other Notch-regulated.

In our model, we observe Notch signaling activation specifically in ISO+sFRP1-treated NRVMs, but not when cells are treated with sFRP1 or ISO independently. This supports the published data that chronic catecholamine stress may positively upregulate Notch signaling via ADAM17-mediated catalytic cleavage, thereby linking the sympathetic stress typical of HF to maladaptive developmental reprogramming47.

Overall, these results suggest that catecholamines act not only as a stressor but also as a molecular modulator, driving how cardiomyocytes convert developmental signals such as WNT and Notch to pathological states that promote iDCM. To suggest a potential crosstalk or relationship between the two pathways in the setting of ISO treatment, we investigated the relationship between Notch and WNT signaling in our model. Down-regulation of β-catenin in ISO+sFPR1-treated cells blunted the effect of Notch activation on NICD protein levels and Notch target gene expression, and chemical inhibition of Notch by DAPT blunted the increase of β-catenin by ISO+sFRP1. Our observation that both pathways are upregulated in ISO+sFRP1-treated cells, rats, and human DCM hearts suggests a cooperative role in driving pathological gene expression programs associated with cardiac dysfunction and ventricular dilation in vivo. These findings support a model in which combined β-adrenergic stimulation and increased WNT signaling activate Notch–WNT signaling, contributing to sustained pathological remodeling and cardiac dysfunction.

sec.title: Notch as a potential therapeutic target in pediatric iDCM.
sec.p: We also show that inhibiting Notch/WNT signaling prevents pathological remodeling. Modulating these pathways could offer age-specific strategies for pediatric HF that differ from adult therapies, especially in diseases like DCM. DAPT, a γ-secretase inhibitor, has shown promise as a potential therapeutic target for many conditions ranging from cancer to cardiovascular diseases. DAPT promotes cardiac reprogramming and has been suggested as a potential therapeutic option for cardiac regeneration48. In this study, we show that DAPT prevents cardiac dysfunction in ISO+sFRP1-treated rats. These findings provide not only mechanistic insight into DCM in children but also a potential therapeutic target for pediatric DCM.

sec.title: Cardiac stiffness as a characteristic of pediatric DCM
sec.p: Additionally, our in vivo model recapitulates other aspects of pediatric DCM, such as no difference in cardiac NPPB levels, minimal fibrosis or lack of myocyte hypertrophy8,17. While fibrosis was not observed in our model, we detected an increase in myocardial stiffness, which recapitulates our prior in vitro studies in cells treated with sFRP112. Notably, both WNT and Notch signaling have been implicated in the regulation of extracellular matrix dynamics and fibroblast activation in adult cardiac fibrosis49; however, in pediatric iDCM, our transcriptomic data suggest these pathways may instead contribute to cardiomyocyte-intrinsic changes and ECM remodeling resulting in stiffness in the absence of classical interstitial fibrosis. The enrichment of ECM pathways in our rat model aligns with this observation and supports the idea that pediatric iDCM may involve dynamic ECM dysregulation without overt fibrotic conversion. Also, ECM remodeling in the absence of overt fibrosis may represent a unique pathogenic mechanism underlying pediatric iDCM. ECM remodeling and myocardial stiffness have been detected in the hearts of children with iDCM5,26. Our results expand on these prior findings and show that stiffness is increased in pediatric iDCM hearts by AFM and may contribute to impaired cardiac function. ECM stiffness is an important cue that usually precedes fibrosis and can act through WNT signaling50. In addition, modulation of Notch signaling regulates substrate stiffness associated with stiffness of the ECM via mechanotransduction41, and may explain the lack of fibrosis but presence of stiffness in pediatric iDCM. Taken together, our findings suggest myocardial stiffness as an important contributor to pediatric iDCM and support WNT and Notch signaling pathways as important drivers of the disease in children.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: Our study identified a novel paradigm in the investigations of pediatric iDCM that consists of a combined activation of WNT and Notch pathways. Through in vitro studies, we established the secretome as an important mediator of disease, which led to the development of an in vivo model of pediatric iDCM. Using this model, we identified co-activation of Notch and WNT in ISO+sFRP1-treated rats and human pediatric iDCM tissue. Mechanistically, in vitro inhibition of Notch and β-catenin signaling prevented pathological remodeling, and in vivo Notch inhibition improved cardiac function. These results emphasize the relevance of our model for studying pediatric-specific mechanisms of HF and suggest that targeting the Notch–WNT axis could offer new therapeutic opportunities. Collectively, our findings provide an opportunity to explore potential novel therapeutic targets in pediatric DCM.

sec.title: LIMITATIONS
sec.p: (1) This study focused on a young model of disease and its relationship to the pediatric heart. Adults were not included in our analysis. (2) The increase in myocardial stiffness justifies the need to explore the potential mechanisms underlying this increase in stiffness. Understanding the processes associated with ECM remodeling will further our understanding of iDCM mechanisms in children and may identify novel approaches to prevent or reverse stiffness. (3) Although our studies suggest Notch inhibition may be a sound approach to prevent the disease process, the effect of inhibiting β-catenin is yet to be investigated. (4) The current study focused on the interplay between β-catenin and Notch. However, other pathways identified in our transcriptomic data may be relevant and can be further explored in future studies.

sec.title: METHODS
sec.p: Detailed methods can be found in the Supplemental Materials.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: We would like to acknowledge the Heart Transplant Team at Children’s Hospital Colorado.

We would also like to acknowledge the University of Colorado hemodynamics core, especially Elizabeth Hardy, for taking all echocardiographic measurements. Finally, we are grateful to the Integrated Physiology Program at the University of Colorado, particularly Dr. Mary Weiser-Evans, the program Director.

sec.title: Sources of Funding
sec.p: This work was supported by NIH K24HL150630 (to CCS), the Colorado CTSA Grant (UL1 TR002535), NIH R01HL156670 (to SDM, BLS, CCS), the Jack Cooper Millisor Chair in Pediatric Heart Disease (to SDM), American Heart Association (AHA) supplement to promote diversity in science (24DIVSUP1281949) (to OON), AHA predoctoral fellowship (25PRE1375864) (to OON), NIH K25HL148386 (to BP), the Ludeman Family Center for Women’s Health Research seed grant (to BP), American Heart Association 23CDA1052411 (to BP) and the generous grants of John Patrick Albright (to BP).

fn.p: None of the authors have a conflict of interest

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: Bulk and single-nucleus RNA sequencing data will be deposited upon acceptance of the manuscript.

caption.p: ISO+sFRP1 promotes pathological remodeling in NRVMs and cardiac dysfunction in vivo.

(A)RT-qPCR of FGP expression in NRVMs treated with vehicle (Control), ISO, sFRP1 or ISO+sFRP1. Expression of NPPA, NPPB, and ratio of MYH6 to MYH7. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. NRVMs treated with 100nM Isoproterenol (ISO) +/− 1 μg/mL human recombinant sFRP1 for 72 hours. n = 8 independent NRVM preps. All groups are log2 transformed.

(B)RT-qPCR of sFRP1 gene expression in NRVMs treated with ISO+sFRP1 compared to vehicle-treated controls. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to controls. n = 6 independent NRVM preps.

(C) Schematic of injection plan, timeline and duration. Rats were treated with 0.1mg/kg/day ISO every other day for the first 5 treatments and every day for the subsequent 7 days. sFRP1 (50μg/kg/day) was co-administered with ISO (or vehicle (PBS, 0.5mM ascorbic acid).
(D) EF of neonatal rats treated with vehicle (Control), ISO, sFRP1 and ISO+sFRP1.
(E) LV vol(s) of neonatal rats treated with vehicle (Control), ISO, sFRP1 andISO+sFRP1.
(F) LVID(s) of neonatal rats treated with vehicle (Control), ISO, sFRP1 and ISO+sFRP1.
(G) LVAW(s) of neonatal rats treated with vehicle (Control), ISO, sFRP1 and ISO+sFRP1.
(H) LVPW(s) of neonatal rats treated with vehicle (Control), ISO, sFRP1 andISO+sFRP1.
Control n = 20, ISO n = 9, sFRP1 n = 9, ISO+sFRP1 n=19. Only significant p-values are notated in the Figure.

Only significant P values (p<0.05) are notated in the Figure. Fitting a mixed model, Tukey’s multiple comparisons test was used for multiple comparison. Unpaired t-test was used for two-group analysis. Error bar denotes mean± SEM. NRVMs, neonatal rat ventricular myocytes; FGP, fetal gene program; ISO, Isoproterenol; sFRP1, secreted frizzled protein 1; EF, ejection fraction; LV vol(s), Left ventricular volume in systole; LVID. Left ventricular internal diameter in systole; LVAW(s), left ventricular anterior wall in systole; LVPW (s), left ventricular posterior wall in systole.
Cardiac stiffness is increased in pediatric DCM hearts and ISO+sFRP1 rats.

(A)Levels of sFRP1 mRNA in left ventricle (LV) tissue of ISO+sFRP1-treated rats compared to controls. Gene expression was measured by RT-qPCR and normalized to 18S. n = 12/group. Data are presented as a relative fold change to NF controls; all data are log2 transformed.
(B)Levels of sFRP1 in serum from ISO+sFRP1 rats compared to controls. Serum levels were measured by ELISA. Control n = 3, ISO+sFRP1 n = 5. ELISA, Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay.
(C-E) Elasticity/stiffness (Young’s modulus) in rats treated with ISO+sFRP1 (D) compared to controls (C). Quantification is shown in (E). n =3 per group.
(F-H) Elasticity/stiffness (Young’s modulus) in explanted pediatric DCM hearts (G) compared to non-failing controls (F). Quantification is shown in (H). NF n =3, DCM n =4.
Unpaired 2-tailed t-test was used in all analyses. Only Significant p-values (p<0.05) are notated in the Figure. Error bar denotes mean ± SEM. NF, non-failing; DCM, dilated cardiomyopathy
Notch and WNT signaling are predicted to be altered in ISO+sFRP1 rats and in pediatric DCM hearts.
(A)Schematic of the experimental plan and bulk RNA-sequencing.
(B)Heatmap representing unsupervised hierarchical clustering of the top 50 significantly differentially expressed genes (DEGs). n = 6/group; Welch’s t test p < 0.05.
(C) Pathway analysis using the 113 upregulated genes in ISO+sFRP1 vs Control ratsusing Reactome pathway analysis tool.
(D) Pathway analysis using all 286 differentially expressed genes in ISO+sFRP1 vs Control rats using Hallmark pathway analysis tool.

(E)Venn diagram representation of overlapping genes altered in pediatric DCM left ventricle (LV) tissue compared to non-failing controls, and in LV tissue from ISO+sFRP1-treated rats compared to controls.

(F) Venn diagram representation of overlapping genes altered in pediatric DCM serum-treated NRVMs compared to cells treated with serum from age-matched non-failing controls, and in LV tissue from ISO+sFRP1-treated rats compared to controls.
(G) Pathway analysis using the upregulated genes shared in serum-treated NRVMs and LV tissue of ISO+sFRP1-treated rats identified by Reactome pathway analysis tool.
DCM, dilated cardiomyopathy; ISO-Isoproterenol, sFRP1− secreted frizzled protein 1; NRVMs, neonatal rat ventricular myocytes; LV, left ventricle.
Single-nuclei RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) identifies activation of Notch and β-catenin signaling in response to ISO+sFPR1.

(A) Representative UMAP plot from single-nuclei RNA sequencing of LV tissue fromControls, ISO−, sFRP1− or ISO+sFRP1-treated rats, n = 3/group. A total of 9214 nuclei in control, 11588 nuclei in ISO−, 13758 nuclei in sFRP1−, and 11127 in ISO+sFRP1-treated rats were identified after cells were pooled. 26 distinct cell clusters were identified including 8 CMs and 5 FBs.
(B) Graphical representation of the percent cell type distribution in vehicle-treatedcontrol rats.
(C) Graphical representation of the percent cell type distribution in ISO+sFRP1 rats.
(D) Graphical representation of the percent ventricular cardiomyocyte sub-clusters invehicle-treated control rats and ISO+sFRP1.
(E) Reactome pathway analysis using upregulated genes in Ventricularcardiomyocyte sub-cluster 2 of ISO+sFRP1 vs Control rats.
(F) Enrichr analysis identifies transcription factors predicted to control expression ofupregulated genes in ventricular cardiomyocyte sub-cluster 2.
(G) Reactome pathways analysis using upregulated genes in ventricularcardiomyocyte sub-cluster 6 of ISO+sFRP1 vs Control rats.
(H) Enrichr analysis identifies transcription factors predicted to control expression ofupregulated genes in ventricular cardiomyocyte sub-cluster 6.
(I) ClinVar database analysis of upregulated genes in the ventricular cardiomyocytecluster.

Clusters were annotated into major cell populations, and the ventricular cardiomyocyte cluster was sub-clustered into eight different sub-clusters using R statistical software. Differentially expressed genes for each cluster were calculated with the FindAllMarkers function. UMAP: uniform manifold approximation and projection, CM: cardiomyocytes; FB: fibroblast; NK: Natural Killer cells; B/T: B cells and T cells
Levels of NICD are increased in nuclear and cytoplasmic fractions in vivo, in vitro and in explanted human DCM hearts.

(A-D) Western blot showing increased protein levels of NICD in the cytoplasm (A) and nuclear fractions (C) of NF vs DCM human LV tissue. (B) and (D) represent quantification of (A) and (C) respectively. Cytoplasmic n=6/group, Nuclear n=7/group.

(E-H) Western blot showing increased protein levels of NICD in the cytoplasm (E) and nuclear fractions (G) of ISO+sFRP1 vs vehicle-treated control rat LV tissue. (F) and (H) represent the quantification of (E) and (G) respectively. Cytoplasmic n=6/group, Nuclear n=7/group.

(I-L) Western blot showing the increased protein levels of NICD in the cytoplasm (I) and nuclear fractions (K) of ISO+sFRP1 vs vehicle-treated NRVMs. (J) and (L) represent the quantification of (I) and (K) respectively. Cytoplasmic n = 4 independent NRVM preps, nuclear n = 5 independent NRVM preps. NICD. Notch Intracellular domain, NRVMs, neonatal rat ventricular myocytes.

(M) RT-qPCR of Notch1 and Hey1 gene expression in ISO+sFRP1 rat LV tissuecompared to Control. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. n=8/group.

(N) RT-qPCR of Notch target genes in NRVMs treated with vehicle (Control) andISO+sFRP1. Expression of Notch1, Hey1, HEY2, HES1 and HEYL genes. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. n = 6 independent NRVM preps/group. NF, non-failing; DCM, dilated cardiomyopathy; NICD, Notch Intracellular domain. Unpaired 2-tailed t test was used in analysis. P-values are notated in each graph.
Levels of β-catenin are increased in nuclear and cytoplasmic fractions in vivo, in vitro and in explanted human DCM hearts.

(A-D) Western blot showing increased protein levels of β-catenin in the cytoplasm (A) and nuclear fractions (C) of NF and DCM explanted human LV tissue. (B) and (D) represent the quantification of (A) and (C) respectively. NF, non-failing; DCM, dilated cardiomyopathy; cytoplasmic n=3/group, nuclear n=7/group.

(E-H) Western blot showing increased protein levels of β-catenin in the cytoplasm (E) and nuclear fractions (G) of ISO+sFRP1 vs vehicle-treated control rat LV tissue. (F) and (H) represent the quantification of (E) and (G) respectively. Cytoplasmic n=3/group, nuclear, n=7/group.

(I-L) Western blot showing increased protein levels of β-catenin in the cytoplasm (I) and nuclear fractions (K) of ISO+sFRP1 vs vehicle-treated NRVMs. (J) and (L) represent the quantification of (I) and (K) respectively. Cytoplasmic and nuclear fractions from n=4 independent NRVM preps.

(M) RT-qPCR of CTNNB1 gene expression in ISO+sFRP1-treated rat LV tissuecompared to Control. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. n=7/group

(N) RT-qPCR of CTNNB1 gene expression in NRVMs treated with vehicle (Control) or ISO+sFRP1. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. n=8 independent NRVM preps/group NRVMs, neonatal rat ventricular myocytes. Unpaired 2-tailed t test was used in analysis. p-values are notated in each graph.
β-catenin contributes to Notch activation, and inhibiting β-catenin prevents pathological remodeling in ISO+sFRP1-treated cells.

(A) RT-qPCR of Notch target gene expression in NRVMs transfected with scrambled control siRNA (scr) or siCTNNB1, and treated with vehicle (Control) or ISO+sFRP1. Expression of Hey1, HEY2, HES1 and HEYL genes. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. n=6 independent NRVM preps/group

(B) RT-qPCR of FGP gene expression in NRVMs transfected with scrambled control siRNA (scr) or siCTNNB1 and treated with vehicle (Control) or ISO+sFRP1. Expression of NPPA, NPPB, and ratio of MYH6 to MYH7. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. n=4 independent NRVM preps/group

(C-F) Western blot showing reduced protein levels of NICD in the cytoplasm and nuclear fractions (E) of NRVMs treated with ISO+sFRP1 and transfected with si-CTNNB1 when compared to ISO+sFRP1-treated cells. (D) and (F) represent the quantification of (C) and (E) respectively. n=3 independent NRVM preps/group. NICD, Notch Intracellular domain, NRVMs, neonatal rat ventricular myocytes
Notch contributes to β-catenin activation, and inhibiting Notch prevents pathological remodeling and cardiac dysfunction.

(A) RT-qPCR of Notch target gene expression (HEY1, HEY2, HES1 and HEYL) in NRVMs treated with vehicle (Control), ISO+sFRP1, DAPT (20μM) or ISO+sFRP1+DAPT. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. n = 7 independent NRVM preps/group

(B) RT-qPCR of FGP in NRVMs treated with vehicle (Control), ISO+sFRP1, DAPT (20μM) or ISO+sFRP1+DAPT. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. n = 5 independent NRVM preps/group

(C) Western blot showing the reduced protein levels of β-catenin in the cytoplasm and nuclear fractions (E) of ISO+sFRP1+DAPT or ISO+sFRP1-treated NRVMs. (D) and (F) represent the quantification of (C) and (E) respectively. P-values are notated in each graph. n = 4 independent NRVM preps/group. NRVM, neonatal rat ventricular myocytes

(G) RT-qPCR of CTNNB1 gene expression in NRVMs treated with vehicle (Control), ISO+sFRP1, DAPT (20μM) and ISO+Sfrp1+DAPT. Gene expression was normalized to 18S, and data are presented as a relative fold change to Controls. Tukey’s multiple comparisons test was used for all data sets. Control, n = 4, ISO+sFRP1 n = 8, DAPT n = 4 and ISO+sFRP1+DAPT n = 7

(H) EF, LV vol(s) and LVID(s) of neonatal rats treated with Control, ISO+sFRP1 and DAPT ISO+sFRP1. Control n=13, ISO+sFRP1 n=13. DAPT+ISO+sFRP1 n=6. EF: ejection fraction; LV vol(s), Left ventricular volume in systole; LVID. Left ventricular internal diameter in systole. Tukey’s multiple comparisons test was used for all data sets. P-values are notated in the graph.

list-item.p: iDCM in children continues to be poorly understood due to the lack of an animal model and lack of understanding of the disease mechanisms.

This study establishes a unique young rat model that recapitulates key features of the disease, which include impaired systolic function with ventricular dilation, myocardial stiffness and distinct transcriptomic signatures.
This study investigates molecular pathways commonly altered in pediatric iDCM heart and our novel animal model.

We identified Notch and WNT co-activation as key regulators of pathological remodeling and cardiac dysfunction, and show that inhibition of Notch signaling can prevent pathological remodeling and cardiac dysfunction, suggesting targeting the Notch pathway may be a viable option to treat pediatric DCM.
These findings provide a preclinical model to investigate targeted interventions for pediatric DCM.
